author: Pradnesh Patil
content:
Ever asked an AI agent to build a data pipeline and watched it hallucinate column names, miss lineage, or generate SQL that doesn’t match your schema?
We built Altimate Code (open source, MIT) to fix that. It plugs into your warehouse + dbt project (models, lineage, schemas) so the agent works with real context and can actually execute dbt tasks end-to-end instead of guessing.
It’s been working well for things like model creation/refactoring, debugging runs, and navigating large projects. We’re seeing ~74.4% on ADE-bench (vs ~65% Cortex Code, ~40% Claude Code), but more interesting is just how it behaves on real projects.
Works with any warehouse + any LLM.
Try it: npm install -g altimate-code
Examples: https://docs.altimate.sh/examples/

author: Sergi Sánchez
content:
hey guys!😄  @here

I am working on a personal project involving a **multi-agent system** where one of the agents has access to several tools.
After developing the whole system, I reached to a point where this agent had to perform good on 2 tasks:
- Task A (*Tool Selection*): the model must choose, from a reduced tool scope, the correct tool and with the correct arguments
- Task B (*Structured Generation*): the model must conditionally generate a structured response based on the tool responses, with precise rules and formats.

So after careful consideration, instead of improving even more the prompting, Few-shot prompting... I considered **fine-tuning** the model to improve on both tasks by generating a dataset with correct task A and B, so we could have 2 huge improvements:
- __Reduce the system prompt size__ drastically, since it does not need to contain tool guidelines, response format&style and rules
- __Improve performance__ of tool selection and final response generation of the ag

author: cute_parrot_36506
content:
Built something for longitudinal PHI de-identification, curious what people think

Most clinical de-identification pipelines I have seen treat each record as its own isolated problem. That works until you are dealing with the same patient across multiple systems over time. A note from last Tuesday looks clean. An imaging report from three months ago looks clean. Put them together with a waveform timestamp and a physician name and suddenly you have something identifying. Nobody's pipeline caught it because nobody was keeping score across records.

That is the problem I spent the last while building around. The system maintains a graph of what has already been exposed for each patient across modalities and uses that history to decide how aggressively to mask each new incoming event. PPO controller, five masking tiers, consent enforced as a hard ceiling. For anyone running multi-site setups the exposure graph syncs as a CRDT so you get consistent privacy state across nodes without needing

author: Burny
content:
We're going over "Can we interpret latent reasoning using current mechanistic interpretability tools?" in the reading group in the mechinterp Discord now!!
Abstract: 
"We study a simple latent reasoning LLM on math tasks using standard mechanistic interpretability techniques to see whether the latent reasoning process (i.e., vector-based chain of thought) is interpretable.
Results:
We find that the model solves maths problems requiring three reasoning steps by storing the two intermediate values in specific latent vectors (the third and fifth of six). We established this using standard mechanistic interpretability techniques.
The logit lens shows that intermediate calculations are represented in the residual stream during latent reasoning.
The latent vectors are not perfectly interpretable via the logit lens, but through patching, we demonstrate that they store intermediate calculations.
Patching has no effect on the model’s accuracy when applied to problems with the same intermediate

author: wave function
content:
@everyone <@&782362139087208478> 

**Paper Discussion 2026#08

<t:1772305200:F>, <@294632311176298498> will present `Towards Long-Horizon Interpretability: Efficient and Faithful Multi-Token Attribution for Reasoning LLMs` in https://discord.com/channels/714501525455634453/1337720641528401930

Event:
https://discord.gg/hTbsb7cF?event=1477357440080281681

**Paper*: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01914

**Abstract**:
Token attribution methods provide intuitive explanations for language model outputs by identifying causally important input tokens. However, as modern LLMs increasingly rely on extended reasoning chains, existing schemes face two critical challenges: (1) efficiency bottleneck, where attributing a target span of M tokens within a context of length N requires O(M*N) operations, making long-context attribution prohibitively slow; and (2) faithfulness drop, where intermediate reasoning tokens absorb attribution mass, preventing importance from propagating back to the original input.

author: herrtindlund
content:
Hey there, my team and I have been fascinated by how devs reason about their codebase and how parts fit together, and we’ve explored whether a generated, interactive, dynamic architecture map is the right reasoning layer for making architectural decisions. That’s why we built Legend - just ingest a codebase of your choosing and get a structured overview of modules, components, and the decisions that connect them. The map is the starting point, as Legend lets you :
edit to define what the system should look like
write specs directly onto components
and export architecture decisions as implementation tickets.

The spec stays alive with the code, instead of today’s whiteboarding which becomes expired after a week
Would love to get your input on what we’ve built so far. Would something like this have helped you get up to speed faster on a new codebase?
Github: https://github.com/LEGEND-LLP/Full_method

author: skelts
content:
Hey everyone! Most of us have dealt with the glue code problem in M, specifically the nightmare of orchestrating real-time features, candidate retrievers, and Learning-to-Rank (L2R) models.

Most teams are still maintaining a fragmented stack: vector DB for retrieval, Redis for features, and thousands of lines of Python scripts to handle the hand-off. It’s a mess of training-serving skew and latency bottlenecks.

We just launched ShapedQL on Product Hunt to collapse this into a single engine (See it here: https://www.producthunt.com/products/shaped/launches/shapedql)

It’s a SQL dialect that compiles declarative queries into a high-performance ranking pipeline. It handles the full MLOps loop natively:
- Online Features: Syncs behavioral streams in seconds to an internal feature store.
- Point-wise Scoring: Low-latency (<50ms) inference for p(click) or p(buy).

author: TheGuy
content:
It is supposed to be supplementary, so probably not many.

It is supposed to be a hub of your medical info. In essence "talk with your data, health edition". A smart doctor, would just take the info as a thing to verify, which if it is referencing real documents, can be cross validated.

As for individuals using it *instead* of doctors, IDK. Couldn't be worse than people already using it for health decisions. And, to LLMs credit, there is a lot of people I know who have used LLMs to catch ailments early that doctors have missed, or piece together a full narrative to point a doctor in the right direction.

My biggest concern is mainly user privacy, and ChatGPT becoming the sort of everything app monopoly. In the least Google with MedGemma open sourced their models.

content:
# Truthound Orchestration & Dashboard

Two new extensions for the Truthound data quality framework.

---

## Truthound Orchestration

Workflow platform integrations for Airflow, Dagster, Prefect, dbt, Mage AI, and Kestra.

Web-based data quality monitoring UI.

author: Doug_Bitterbot
content:
We just open-sourced our training stack for a recursive transformer (TOPAS-DSPL - 24% on ARC-AGI-2 public data set!!!).

The interesting engineering challenge was stabilizing the gradients across the dual-stream recursion. We ended up using MuonClip and a custom Test-Time Training (TTT) pipeline to get it to converge.

It’s hitting 24% on ARC with only 15M parameters. If anyone is working on recursive architectures or TTT pipelines, I’d love feedback on our implementation.

Repo/code for independent validation: https://github.com/Bitterbot-AI/topas_DSLPv1

6️⃣ Synthetic data governance becomes non-negotiable
7️⃣ AI governance moves to the boardroom
8️⃣ Workflow redesign beats model tuning every time

The uncomfortable truth running through all eight: AI didn’t fail because the models weren’t good enough. It failed because the foundations weren’t built for autonomous systems.

author: [Deet]Isaac
content:
That sounds about right.

All technical features/capabilities are a function of market/customer need, ROI and business value.

Whether it is about stock price/investor trust and excitement, customer satisfaction, beating out a competitor, etc, features and large capabilities go through a long (or short depending on the company) process of product fit, ROI estimation, product need, and marketing analysis.

Basically, you have to prove that customers want your enhancement or it provides some value to the company that is greater than its cost.

An ML model which increases sales conversion by .03% could end up being hundreds of thousands of extra revenue over a year. Even if it costs a hundred thousand to develop, it still provides way more value to the company than it costs. 

Similarly, the ML model that delivers a lot of “wow factor” to the investors meeting may unlock another million in funding, even if the ML model does not have ROI from a sales standpoint, it paid out in investor int

author: skelts
content:
Hey everyone,

I’m really proud to share what my team and I have been building. We’ve spent the last few years trying to solve the 'Frankenstein Stack' problem in personalization (where you have to glue together Elastic, Pinecone, Redis, and Python services just to get a ranked list).
We decided to build a Relevance Engine from the ground up to collapse all those layers into one.

The cool technical stuff we shipped today:
- Vertically Integrated: We run the Vector Index and the ML Inference in the same memory space. Latency is <50ms p99.
- SQL Pipeline: We mapped the 4-stage recommender pipeline (Retrieve → Filter → Score → Rank) to a SQL-like dialect.
- Zero-ETL: It ingests Kafka streams and Postgres tables and handles the joins at query time so you don't have to flatten data.
- We effectively moved the 'Feature Store' inside the 'Search Engine' to make relevance stateful by default.

We just launched a free dev tier ($300 credits). I’d love for the folks here to break it and tell me

author: flora131
content:
Hi guys! A little while ago we open-sourced our workflow through ‘agent-instructions’. Since then we've been iterating and seeing how agents lack principled software engineering practices and memory across sessions. Ultimately, we were spending a lot of time debugging the agent instead of building with it because it wouldn’t follow our instructions and have cascading failures.

So we rebuilt it as Atomic which has specs that persist as memory, small atomic features (way more reliable) with active memory during implementation, and engineer-in-the-loop as a first principle.

Repo: https://github.com/flora131/atomic Deep dive: https://alexlavaee.me/blog/atomic-workflow/

We ended up documenting this because it’s the way we’ve been building our side projects. Sharing in case it’s helpful to other folks in the community. 

Built on learnings from Dex Horthy/Humanlayer, Vaibhav Gupta/Boundary, Geoff Huntley, Jesse Vincent, and the Claude Code + Codex’s Aaron Friel and team. If you haven’t ch

author: CCB -TK
content:
Hey everyone,

I’m working at a robotics / physical AI startup and we’re getting ready to release step-by-step a developer-facing Computer Vision API library.

It exposes a set of pretrained and finetunable models for robotics and automation use cases, including:

- 6D object pose estimation
- 2D/3D object detection
- Instance & semantic segmentation
- Anomaly detection
- Point cloud processing
- Model training / fine-tuning endpoints
- Deployment-ready inference APIs

Our goal is to make it easier for CV/robotics engineers to prototype and deploy production-grade perception pipelines without having to stitch together dozens of repos.

We want to share this with the community to:

- collect feedback,
- validate what’s useful / not useful for you,
- and iterate before a wider release.

My question:
Where would you recommend sharing tools like this to reach CV engineers and robotics developers?

If anyone here wants early access to try some of the APIs, drop a comment and I’ll DM you.

author: AER Labs
content:
**AER: Accelerate Efficient/Effective Research | AI Engineering & Research**

The most critical bottleneck in the AI revolution isn't compute. It's access.

If the frontier of intelligence—tools like GPT-5—is gated behind a $200/month paywall, we are engineering a cognitive caste system. OpenAI is now reportedly planning to charge up to $20,000 per month for specialized AI agents aimed at "PhD-level research." This jaw-dropping figure underscores a deeper problem: we are effectively deciding who gets to participate in the future and who gets left behind. That is an unacceptable outcome.

**AER Labs is here to unblock the stack.**

We are building the open-source infrastructure necessary to democratize intelligence. We aren't just consumers; we are architects of the inference layer, building in the spirit of vLLM and SGLang.

Our thesis is simple: Talent is globally distributed, but opportunity is not. We are hunting for "Dark Talent"—the outliers and high-agency engineers in Korea, Ind

Step-by-step verifiers -- also known as process reward models (PRMs) -- are a key ingredient for test-time scaling. PRMs require step-level supervision, making them expensive to train. This work aims to build data-efficient PRMs as verbalized step-wise reward models that verify every step in the solution by generating a verification chain-of-thought (CoT). We propose ThinkPRM, a long CoT verifier fine-tuned on orders of magnitude fewer process labels than those required by discriminative PRMs. Our app

Hello Everyone ,
I need to share a struggle that I know will resonate deeply with anyone seriously trying to do Edge AI: the constant, agonizing question of picking the right SBC (compute and GPU) for doing Computer Vision at EDGE.

My team and I have wasted so much time and money buying Jetson Nano, RPi then realizing it was underpowered, then shelling out for an Orin, only to find out it was overkill. We had multiple use cases, but we couldn't properly prototype or stress-test our models before spending hundreds of dollars for individual boards and spending the first few days/weeks just setting things up. A bigger nightmare was end-of-life and availability of support. It kills momentum and makes the entire prototyping phase feel like a gamble.

Our Fix: Making Users Life Easier and Quicker
We decided we were done with the guesswork. This frustration is why we put our heads down and developed the NVIDIA Edge AI Cloud Lab.

The core mission is simple: we want to quicken the prototyping

author: CireNeikual
content: ART is a framework that avoids forgetting by matching bottom-up inputs to top-down expectations. It selects cells to learn via a special competitive activation function, that implements a "resonance loop" to find the "most active matching cell" (constrained maximum). It's a type of vector quantization and prediction system.

author: CireNeikual
content:
<@695420777771565083> There is another way to achieve truly permanent LTM, that is to only update parts of it very sparsely, but this is at odds with differentiability. In DL networks, we can slow down the learning, but it's kind of uniform as the networks are dense. This leads to memory loss on non-current-task information. Hierarchical timescales can help, but they never reach the "0 forgetting" we want since all memories are being "touched" (updated) every step.

However, there are methods that never forget that already exist already and may serve as a useful basis. For LTM, I am interested in Adaptive Resonance Theory (ART). By itself it is very simple, and kind of useless, but it demonstrates a method that fundamentally solves forgetting for LTM. Fortunately, it is possible to extend the method and upgrade it in many different ways.

author: Harly
content:
Hello everyone,

Just want to share my project

Polyp Segmentation System MLOps project.
🚀Polyp Segmentation System MLOps — an end-to-end MLOps platform for medical image segmentation, designed to detect and segment colorectal polyps from endoscopic images.
This project goes far beyond traditional model training. It demonstrates how modern MLOps architecture can automate the entire machine learning lifecycle — from data ingestion, distributed training, and experiment tracking, all the way to deployment, monitoring, and continuous delivery.

💡 Key Highlights:
Training & Optimization: Ray Train + Ray Tune for distributed training & hyperparameter tuning.
Experiment Tracking: MLflow (PostgreSQL + MinIO backend).
Deployment: KServe with Triton Inference Server on GKE.
Orchestration: Kubeflow Pipelines + Jenkins CI/CD.
Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana for real-time observability.
Infrastructure as Code: Terraform & Helm for reproducible setup.
Check it out on GitHub and drop a ⭐ if you like

author: AntoineN
content:
Hi all! I wanted to share a local LLM playground I made called Apples2Oranges (https://github.com/bitlyte-ai/apples2oranges) that let's you compare models side by side (of different quants, families) just like OpenAI model playground or Google AI Studio. It also comes with hardware telemetry. Though if you're data obsessed, you use it as a normal inference GUI with all the visualizations.

It's built with Tauri + React + Rust and while is currently only compatible with mac (all telemetry is designed to interface with macos) but we will be adding Windows support.

It currently uses rust bindings for llama.cpp (llama-cpp-rs), however we are open to experimenting with different inference engines depending on community wants. It runs models sequentially, and you can set it to automatically wait for hardware cooldown for robust comparisons.

author: Elvira/palitra.ai
content:
Hi everyone, We’ve just launched https://palitra.ai/  - our new experimental platform where AI learns to keep secrets, and the community gets rewarded for trying to break them. How it works: 
**Red Mode:** An agent receives a secret (a 32-byte string), and its hash is published. The challenge for participants is to “convince” the model to reveal the string in chat.
**Blue Mode:** Once a leak occurs, the defense stage begins. The community proposes patches (prompts, filters, defender models) and stress-tests them. The most resilient patch becomes a master patch and the agent returns to Red Mode.
This creates an endless attack–defense cycle: attacks reward participants, resilient patches earn commission for their authors, agents continuously improve by training on the collected dataset.
The longer an agent holds its secret, the more valuable the game becomes. We’re beta-testing and inviting community input and collaborations — everyone’s welcome.

author: Clara Shao
content:
here is my abs `An emerging approach to modeling multivariate time series is to represent them
as a graph, with time series as nodes and pairwise temporal correlations as edges.
Recent advances in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown strong perfor-
mance by assuming a static graph topology and aggregating information from
neighboring time series based on their correlations. In this work, we investigate
the representation power of GNNs for short- and long-term forecasting under both
static and dynamic correlation scenarios, i.e., when pairwise correlations remain
fixed or evolve over time. We show that many popular GNNs exhibit poor gener-
alization in these settings and are even outperformed by structure-agnostic base-
lines. To address this limitation, we introduce three design components—high-
order covariance, dual graph aggregation, and exponentially forgetting estima-
tion—that enhance learning in dynamic correlation scenarios while maintaining
strong performance under static c

author: Abbasid
content:
I’ve read through your conversation, and tbh it looks like you’re the one arguing semantics, which is why I said "you arent demonstrating your point effectively".

>>Calling this “just triangulation” is like calling back-propagation just running the chain rule backwards on a graph.

But it is literally Triangulation in volumetric space the same parallax constraint is being enforced. That’s exactly what feature matching relies on, and voxel carving is just applying it discretely. 
you seem to understand triangulation as just the intersecting of two rays, which in photogrammetry it is not just that, its the broader geometric principle, By your logic, stereo vision wouldn’t be triangulation either, which doesn’t hold up.
There’s nothing novel here, and I get that you didnt claim it was novel, 
Honestly, it seems like you dont fully get what triangulation and the parallax constraint/epipolar geometry fully cover.

author: christodouloskech
content:
Hello everyone!

Very excited about the new toolkit we've built: Cross-Domain Saliency Maps ✨

**Cross-domain Saliency Maps** goes beyond time-domain saliency for time-series ML models. Instead of only point-wise importance in time, it can generate attributions in any domain expressed as an invertible differentiable transform, e.g. frequency or ICA – which often makes explanations more interpretable. 

⚡️ Works plug-and-play with PyTorch & Tensorflow (no retraining needed)
💻 Code: https://github.com/esl-epfl/cross-domain-saliency-maps
📄 Paper: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.13100
🔬Examples:

author: XBastille
content:
Hey everyone 👋 , 

I'm super excited to finally share that my project, DeepFX Studio, is complete! It's a web platform I've been building with my team that reproduces a bunch of cool computer vision models like DeOldify for colorization, Real-ESRGAN for upscaling, etc and we have integrated advanced Inpainting such as LaMa for object removal and `alimama-creative/flux.1-dev-controlnet-inpainting-beta`  for fill/replacement via diffusers and SAM for masking  and more, all wrapped in a user-friendly interface. From September 12–14, 2025, teams build on the Cua Agent Framework and are scored in HUD’s OSWorld-Verified environment to push past today’s SOTA on OS-World.

On-site (Track A)
Build during the weekend and submit a repo with a one-line start command. HUD executes your command in a clean environment and runs OSWorld-Verified. author: walpurgisnacht
content:
need advice, i think i have made a pretty fatal career mistake and now im paying for it. 

Put short, all my career (almost 5 years) was spent on RnD (in AI/ML) of a possible new product, but seldom making it into production, or seldom reaching it into an actual depth. Earlier into my career I also lack direction / a good mentor, so I'm usually pretty lost on my own. 

Make a prototype, business direction changed, make another prototype, and so on, and so on. Throughout the projects I often has to switch domain, so I have a lot of experience that goes wide but not deep. author: AvаиτGаRde¹⁹¹⁷
content:
Let this sink in. 

**In 2024 - to generate the Dream-encoded CLIP embeddings Visually - for the Monumental and revolutionary work of demonstrating the recording and rendering of a Dream -  in Video format**

**They used Gradient descent on noisy pixels in Full Scale 2D Pixel Space** 😭 

**The Process They Chose, in All Its Absurdity:**
1.  Get dream CLIP vector from fMRI brain data 🧠
2. Start with raw noise pixels 📺
3. CLIP whispers “no... closer...” every iteration ☁️
4. Adjust pixels slightly, re-ask CLIP, repeat 100+ times Per Frame until anything resembling a cat begins to take shape. Eventually it's as good as it's going to get, a pixel can only move so far. 
5. They cut their losses and move on to the next one until they finally have 5000 pseudo-converged frames (roughly 5 min of content) 
6. This would take an absurd amount of time.

**Meanwhile, Reality Offered:**
- VQGAN+CLIP: “Hi, I’m latent, fast and elegant. Optimize my weights instead? I have discriminator class loss, cos

author: sharathr
content:
🔥 Ramparts Launch Announcement! 🔥
We just open-sourced Ramparts — our security scanner for the Model Context Protocol (MCP): https://lnkd.in/gteYQSD7
If you’re watching LLM agents explode inside your org, the risky surface isn’t the model—it’s the tool interface. MCP lets agents read files, access internal APIs, run SQL… and that means classic abuse paths (path traversal, command/SQL injection) plus new mcp vulnerabilities like tool poisoning, mcp rug pull attacks and multi-step chains you only see when you reason across tools.
Ramparts scans an MCP server, enumerates every capability, and flags higher-order abuse paths. Same binary we run internally—now open to everyone. If your team uses Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code, Codex - you may already be using MCP servers without knowing how secure they may be!
Its in active development, stars, comments, PRs, feedback welcome!
Open Source Repo: https://github.com/getjavelin/ramparts
Read our launch blog here: https://www.getjavelin.com/blogs/r

author: pr0mila
content:
Hey everyone! 🎉
I’ve been working on something exciting, and I’m finally ready to share it with you all. I’ve fine-tuned a Text-to-Speech (TTS) model called Medibeng-Orpheus-3b-0.1-ft, specifically built to handle Bengali-English code-switching in healthcare scenarios. It’s designed to generate speech for patient-doctor interactions where both languages are commonly used.
What makes it special?
- It can seamlessly switch between Bengali and English, which is great for bilingual healthcare environments.
- The model’s been fine-tuned on the MediBeng dataset, which includes simulated synthetic healthcare dialogues.
- Huge shoutout to Unsloth for speeding up the training process—made it way faster than expected! ⚡

I’ve also got some cool stuff for you to check out:

author: tceti
content:
I would recommend looking into early stage startups. Generally they have a lower experience bar (since it’s harder to attract talent) and tend to be understaffed which leads to opportunity for high ownership (even if it’s more work).
You’ll just have to be okay with likely little benefits and working a lot and the idea being risky. But I’d recommend optimizing for growth when starting out.

Some tips when searching. Do your homework on the funding rounds they’ve done and also look into the founders and their track record. I like to look for someone who is extremely enthusiastic about their idea, with a CTO with obvious core competence. You could test that by doing some research on the product and asking them about it, to see how well versed they are in the field. Early stage startup success generally strongly depends on the motivation will and energy of the founders. If you don’t get to meet any founder before making a decision that’s a red flag to me. If a founder “feels” sketchy that

author: Horde
content:
"And that is the best case scenario, for every Dwarf Fortress there are thousands of games barely selling at all. If you want to be an Indie Dev, don't go into it with the expectations of making a living off of it."

1. Indeed, not just thousands. Nearly all. The downside.

At least half of games that release make nothing at all. Of the remainder, you can estimate what they made and it's $2K-$5K.

Then about 500 each year or 1 percent make anything meaningful (full time salary for one engineer). 100-200 each year generate most of the revenue.

That is the main reason it might not be worth it. The downside.

Finally each year more games release. 2026 will have more games release than 2025, which is on track to beat 2024. There is an overall glut. It will only increase.

If you make games slowly, you will *almost certainly* not succeed.

2. The upside. If you don't make games slowly, but make them quickly, the risk is rather different. 

Most of the competition isn't competition, if you

author: kram
content:
Dwarf Fortress was such a special case that's been in development for *decades* before launching a paid version. Before that, it was loosely community funded, with a very slowly built audience of loyal fans who chose to give a little despite it being free.

Yeah you can theoretically become the next Indie Darling Millionaire
No it's not even remotely likely. It's no different from playing the lottery except that with making games it's hard work to even just get a ticket that may or may not win.

The vast majority of games see like no ROI and they are hard work because you need such broad sets of skills. Potentially (depending on the game in question of course) pretty much every single artform may come together to bring forth any given game.

If you got the spare time and drive, if nothing else, making a game would be a fast way to have you teach yourself loads of new skills, but don't go in with the actual expectation that you'll definitely be sustainably successful

**Abstract**:  The most widely used generative models map noise and data distributions by matching flows or scores. However, they struggle to incorporate partial observations and additional priors--something energy-based models (EBMs) handle elegantly by simply adding corresponding scalar energy terms. author: wave function
content:
In <t:1750379400:R>, we'll discuss https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.10612 , by <@1366309193526804573> , in the [Daily Paper Discussion Voice Channel](https://discord.com/channels/714501525455634453/1045298343896690699).

# Energy Matching: Unifying Flow Matching and Energy-Based Models for Generative Modeling

**Abstract**:  The most widely used generative models map noise and data distributions by matching flows or scores. However, they struggle to incorporate partial observations and additional priors--something energy-based models (EBMs) handle elegantly by simply adding corresponding scalar energy terms. We address this issue by proposing Energy Matching, a framework that endows flow-based approaches with the flexibility of EBMs. Far from the data manifold, samples move along curl-free, optimal transport paths from noise to data. As they approach the data manifold, an entropic energy term guides the system into a Boltzmann equilibrium distribution, explicitly capturing the underlying lik

content:
[This paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.08757) talks about the technical details of this system if anyone's interested.
And more importantly, [this paper](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3706598.3713365), presented at CHI'25, contains the results of a user study in a pool of users who use LLMs in the daily life for different types of workflows (technical, business consulting, etc...). We are working on improving the system further based on the feedback received.


I wanted to put this project out here and ask the community for feedback. So, feel free to let me know what you all think about this system / project as a whole (be as critical as you want to be), suggest features you would like to see, point out things that are frustrating, identify other potential use-cases that we might have missed, etc...

🚀 Here is a [demo](https://huggingface.co/spaces/santanavagner/responsible-prompting-demo) hosted on HuggingFace that you can try out this project in. Edit the prompt to start seeing rec

author: MysticSlice
content:
Hi everyone!
I am an intern at IBM Research in the Responsible Tech team.
We are working on an open-source project called the Responsible Prompting API. This is the [Github](https://github.com/IBM/responsible-prompting-api).

It is a lightweight system that provides recommendations to tweak the prompt to an LLM so that the output is more responsible (less harmful, more productive, more accurate, etc...) and all of this is done pre-inference. This separates the system from the existing techniques like alignment fine-tuning (training time) and guardrails (post-inference).

The team's vision is that it will be helpful for domain experts with little to no prompting knowledge. They know what they want to ask but maybe not how best to convey it to the LLM. So, this system can help them be more precise, include socially good values, remove any potential harms.
Again, this is only a recommender system...so, the user can choose to use or ignore the recommendations.

This system will also help t

author: [Deet]Isaac
content:
Cost, evaluation/explainability in regulated spaces, time for inference is leaps and bounds longer than almost any traditional method, less understanding of input->output impact or understanding of how data changes results, etc.

Don’t get me wrong, we use LLMs for a ton of stuff, and rightfully so. And I think they excel at being a POC for NLP as well. For example we used one for a classification use case, to prove that classification could solve some problems we were having, and then we built a model that was more reliable, could have a more robust MLOps pipeline around, was much cheaper at runtime, and was near instantaneous removing the lag caused by the LLM prototype

content:
A bit of background: in my day-to-day work, I typically receive a prototype model from the Data Science team, and my responsibility is to productionize it. This includes building pipelines for:
    •   Feature collection and feature engineering
    •   Model training and retraining
    •   Inference pipelines
    •   Monitoring data drift and model drift
    •   Dockerizing and deploying to Kubernetes clusters
    •   Setting up supporting data infrastructure like feature stores
    •   Building experiment tracking and A/B testing pipelines

This has been my core focus for a long time, and my background is more rooted in data engineering.

Lately, I’ve been interviewing for MLOps roles, and I’ve noticed that the interviews vary wildly in focus. author: darkmabler
content:
Hi all!

I’ve been exploring some ideas around modeling cognition geometrically, and I’ve recently gotten pulled into the work of Peter Scholze on condensed mathematics. It started with me thinking about how to formalize learning and reasoning as traversal across stratified combinatorial spaces, and it’s led to some really compelling connections.

Specifically, I’m wondering whether cognition could be modeled as something like a stratified TQFT in the condensed ∞-topos of combinatorial reasoning - where states are structured phases (e.g. learned configurations), and transitions are cobordism-style morphisms that carry memory and directionality. The idea would be to treat inference not as symbol manipulation or pattern matching, but as piecewise compositional transformations in a noncommutative, possibly ∞-categorical substrate.

I’m currently prototyping a toy system that simulates cobordism-style reasoning over simple grid transitions (for ARC), where local learning rules are stitched

content:
In 30 minutes, we're gonna have a 1 hour session about Statistical Physics in Deep Learning:
https://meet.google.com/nvg-ptgt-ucf

Join us if interested!

**Weight Watcher and a Semi-Empirical Theory Of Learning**

The WeightWatcher project is a long-standing research project to provide both a theoretical basis for Why DL works, as well as a practical open-source tool for DL practitioners to develop better AI models. In this talk, we will review the theoretical underpinnings of the theory behind this popular tool (currently over 175K downloads), including both the Theory of Heavy Tailed Self Regularization and the more recently developed SemiEmpirical Theory of Learning, which derives the conditions for ideal learning of a NN. author: viv2668
content:
⚔️ **From Data Tyranny to Data Democracy**: How Risk-Based Governance Frameworks and Data Product Owners can transform Data Tyranny into agile, scalable Data Democratization.

📃 In this article, community author Francesco dig into a shift every data leader should be thinking about:

- From rigid, centralized control ("data tyranny")
- To agile, risk-aware data democratization.

But this shift only works if we change how we think about data:

- Treat it like a long-term product, not just an asset.
- Push ownership left—closer to where data is created.
- Adopt risk-based governance that scales with context (not all data needs the same level of scrutiny).

If your governance model slows down delivery or bottlenecks innovation, it’s time to rethink it.

📩 **Read the complete article here**: https://moderndata101.substack.com/p/from-data-tyranny-to-data-democratization

author: Deleted User
content: Should Data Scientists be involved in coding pre-processing steps of model-related part of request in model serving? Especially when models are evolving and feature set and fields are inherently interrelated. Most people on my team say no and I disagree. I think that releases of new versions  of model challenger model to e.g. MLFlow should only happen when it's tested against pre-processing logic and example requests of up to date input schema. It might be challenging when inference server is not python, but what do you think?

author: vendra99
content:
Our Autonomous FineTuning System is now live, an advanced framework that minimizes manual oversight in

SmallLanguageModel (SLM) optimization. By leveraging multi agent orchestration, robust hierarchical taxonomies, and LLM-based “judges,” this architecture accelerates data augmentation, parallelises fine-tuning, and provides near-human evaluations at scale.

Multi-Agent Coordination

- Employs specialized agents to classify tasks, synthesize high-fidelity data, and run domain-specific validations.
- Streamlines text transformation and code-generation workflows with dynamic task assignments.

Distributed Fine-Tuning

- Manages multiple training jobs simultaneously through dynamic resource allocation.
- Ensures efficient use of computational infrastructure, even under fault conditions.

 LLM-Powered Evaluation

- Integrates LLM “judges” for granular, human-aligned assessments.
- Produces nuanced feedback across correctness, coherence, and task adherence without relying on manual scoring

author: Gabriela Rincón
content:
Hey everyone! 👋 Job Posting: Prompt Engineer - AI Agents - AI SOP & Workflow Specialist


We seek a Prompt Engineer for AI Agents with expertise in SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) writing and workflow documentation.

This role is NOT just about writing good prompts—the AI handles that. Instead, we need someone to design structured workflows, define edge cases, and ensure AI Agents make the right decisions based on user inputs.

What You’ll Do

As our Prompt Engineer (AI SOP & Workflow Specialist), you will:

✅ Develop SOPs & Process Documentation: Write detailed, step-by-step SOPs that guide AI agents in executing tasks accurately.
 ✅ Map Workflows & Identify Edge Cases: Ensure AI knows when to use specific tools (e.g., Google Calendar vs. ClickUp for task tracking).
 ✅ Refine AI Decision-Making: Test and optimize AI outputs to improve accuracy and efficiency.
 ✅ Collaborate with AI Engineers & Business Teams: Work closely with developers and stakeholders to refine automation proces

author: dalmia_man
content:
🚀  SensAI: open-source AI-first learning platform

Sharing something I've been building for the last 5 months. At HyperVerge Academy (https://academy.hyperverge.org/), we help learners from under-resourced backgrounds in India build skills and get jobs in tech. Reviewing their work and providing meaningful feedback at scale was a challenge. To solve it, we have built SensAI - a learning platform built for and with AI from the start.

Learners get an AI coach which gives them instant, personalized feedback that encourages them to think deeply by asking questions, instead of giving away the answers.

Educators can create cohorts, design entire courses using AI, assess text, code and speech, and use AI-generated insights to identify learning gaps and provide personalized support.

It is already being used for upskilling on programming and logical reasoning, preparing for interviews and training new team members. SensAI is open-source. We run a small community of builders in AI + Education

content:
✨ Excited to share a personal project I’ve been working on! ✨
The idea started with a simple yet recurring challenge: improving the recall of bookmarked blogs and articles saved on GitHub. I’ve struggled to quickly retrieve relevant information from my growing collection of links.
To tackle this, I built a tool that lets me seamlessly search and chat with my saved bookmarks, making the process faster and more intuitive.

I focused on extracting meaningful variables from the content, like metadata, to make searches more precise and insightful. Meilisearch became my go-to for handling fast and flexible search queries.
💬 Chat:
For a conversational experience, I turned to Qdrant, enabling me to ask questions and retrieve answers from my saved content in real time.

Here's how I structured the Application:

1️⃣ Data Enrichment: Extracted metadata and generated embeddings to make searches go beyond just link titles.

author: zelosss
content:
Bit of an inquiry here, I've kind let the proof above to simmer for a bit.
I have a behavior cloning + DAgger algo running(for cs285 stuff). My policy currently is continous, and basically samples actions from a normal distribution, where the location is trained from 2-layer simple FNN, and a scale parameter that is also backprop trainable
Now they set the scale parameter to logstd instead of std(reasonable), but apparently whenever backprop runs, it sends values in logstd to negative(which isn't correct, and I have no idea why it goes negative). I've raised each value to e^array of logstd values, but the performance is a lot more horrible than simply returning the mean parameter instead of a distribution
any tips?

author: jonnor
content:
I like to build systems that automatically analyze sensor data with machine learning. Preferably running directly on a microcontroller, to enable efficient, low-cost solutions that can operate stand-alone. Here is a recent project I did just for fun: automated toothbrushing timer, using an accelerometer and some very simple ML.
This is a demo project for emlearn-micropython, a machine learning and digital signal processing library for MicroPython.
Quick demo video: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/U8TeewQ9t-k

Anyone else working on similar things - applying ML to sensor data - running on tiny systems, etc?

author: jonnor
content:
Hi all. Jon here. I work primarily on machine learning for sensor data. Some of that is time-series analysis in the cloud, with IoT connected sensors. And some is machine learning (inference) running done directly on embedded system / microcontroller. Those systems are under 1 watt, 1 MB RAM/ROM - and frequently under 1 milliwatt for the battery powered stuff. What people tend to call "TinyML". Have a background in electronics engineering, digital signal processing and software going back 15 years, and data science / machine learning the last 6 years. Github with some projects, including an ML library for microcontrollers and various public learning resources I keep: https://github.com/jonnor

Is there any channel here where sensor-data, very resource contrained systems like microcontrollers, is the focus (a quick scan did not reveal so much)?

author: noahho
content:
[ShowYC / Nature] https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42647343

Happy to release of TabPFN v2, a tabular foundation model that delivers state-of-the-art predictions on small datasets in just 2.8 seconds for classification and 4.8 seconds for regression compared to strong baselines tuned for 4 hours. Published in Nature, this model outperforms traditional methods on datasets with up to 10,000 samples and 500 features.

Base Model: https://github.com/PriorLabs/tabpfn
API Client: https://github.com/PriorLabs/tabpfn-client

TabPFN v2 is trained on 130 million synthetic tabular prediction datasets to perform in-context learning and output a predictive distribution for the test data points. Each dataset acts as one meta-datapoint to train the TabPFN weights with SGD. As a foundation model, TabPFN allows for fine-tuning, density estimation and data generation.

Compared to TabPFN v1, v2 now natively supports categorical features and missing values. TabPFN v2 performs just as well on datasets

author: Gordy
content:
It's a bit less academic, but after watching a few OpenAI demos where the LLM does cool stuff with the UI, I went and built my own for an apartment-hunting use case. There's a live demo at https://demo.hanakano.com. This demo bot leverages tools for things like:
- Map search
- Adding/Removing map pins
-  Searching a MLS database to answer user questions
- Automatically filtering listings and rendering results to a list
-  Scheduling viewings via 3rd party Google Calendar Appointments page

Overall it was a great way to get my hands dirty with some application-level AI Engineering. My favorite thing that I had to figure out was the separation between server-side and client-side tools. Things like UI state exist only client-side, while database access should be managed server-side, but the LLM doesn't care and will call any tool whenever it wants. Figuring out the separation of data and control flow to handle was an unexpected but enjoyable job.

author: DynamoDZ
content: I think the paper touches on an important safety consideration during alignment fine-tuning, as it highlights how prefilling tokens can inadvertently lead to bypassing intended behavior constraints. Especially, the findings of the first few tokens got the most parameter updates is something new and interesting. While the technique itself isn’t particularly innovative, the demonstration of how easily such jailbreak-like behavior can occur raises concerns about the robustness of alignment mechanisms.

content:

**Paper Discussion 2024#43**

Saturday, 7 PM to 9 UTC (local:  <t:1733598000:F> - <t:1733605200:t>), <@699606075023949884> will present `Safety Alignment Should be Made More Than Just a Few Tokens Deep` in <#719652499740688404>.

If you'd like to present a paper, send a direct message to <@178150411545346049>  with a link to the paper and availability information.

**Paper:**
https://openreview.net/forum?id=6Mxhg9PtDE&s=09

**Abstract:**
The safety alignment of current Large Language Models (LLMs) is vulnerable. Simple attacks, or even benign fine-tuning, can jailbreak aligned models. We note that many of these vulnerabilities are related to a shared underlying issue: safety alignment can take shortcuts, wherein the alignment adapts a model's generative distribution primarily over only its very first few output tokens. We unifiedly refer to this issue as shallow safety alignment. In this paper, we present case studies to explain why shallow safety alig

author: Marie Owens
content:
Hello Everyone! 👋🏼 

🌟 We have our last webinar of the year coming up! 🌟

Join us on Tuesday, December 3rd at 8 A.M. PT for "Building an Enterprise-Scale Feature Store with Featureform and Databricks," featuring our founder, Simba Khadder!

The term "Feature Store" might sound like just a place to store features, but in reality, it’s a powerful system for defining, managing, and deploying large-scale data pipelines. This session will simplify feature stores by breaking down the three main types and showing how they fit into an ML ecosystem. We’ll explore how feature stores enable data scientists to build, manage, and scale their pipelines, even at petabyte levels, while handling streaming data and ensuring versioning and lineage. We’ll also look under the hood to see how Featureform achieves this scale using Apache Iceberg, so you leave with actionable insights to improve your ML platforms and projects.

Toward the end of the session, there will also be time for Q&A.

🫅🏼 Who is it for:

author: lp
content:
Very excited to share our new paper "Benchmarking Vision, Language, & Action Models on Robotic Learning Tasks"

We evaluate how well VLM & VLA models can control robots across 20 different real-world robotics tasks.

This work was done with an incredible team Pranav Guruprasad, Jaewoo Song Yangyue Wang and Paul Liang

Full details, including code and paper links at https://multinet.ai/static/pages/Multinetv01.html

Some highlights below ⬇️

• We compared three leading models (GPT-4o, OpenVLA, JAT) on their ability to handle diverse robotics tasks - from simple pick-and-place to complex kitchen manipulation. Some fascinating results about what works (and what doesn't.) 

• ALL models struggle with complex tasks requiring multiple steps. Simple pick-and-place tasks are easier to transfer to than Complex Manipulation tasks, which exhibit major challenges. 

• We also introduce a novel prompt engineering framework designed by Kyle to effectively map VLMs to other modalities. Check it out h

author: Roger Ng, MD
content:
🤖 Analysis of SelfCodeAlign Paper
Core Innovation:
Instead of using RAG to pull knowledge, it extracts programming concepts from seed examples to generate new tasks. This is truly unique and is faster because search is built into LLMs but not in RAG where vector search is not perfect and you still have to choose many examples to look over again to find the right one.In this way, the model identifies concepts itself rather than matching predefined knowledge, enabling more creative task generation.

Compute Details (from Appendix C.4):

Primary work: 4x NVIDIA A100 GPUs, 128 cores, 512GB RAM
DeepSeek experiments: 8x NVIDIA H100 GPUs
Training configs varied by model size:

StarCoder2-15B: Single A100 (memory constraints)
Others: 4-GPU training with PyTorch DDP

Key Points:

No explicit scoring of extracted concepts - quality measured through final model performance
Built-in validation through code execution & test cases
Heavy reliance on high-quality seed examples
Lacks concept-level qual

author: andreaskoepf
content:
IMO the video is very  misleading, i.e. the paper uses GPT-4o to generate python programs for GSM-8k + parameters which allows to generate variations of the original questions this is then used to analyze how sensitive different llms are to the perturbations in the questions and the authors find that changing the parameters in the questions significantly reduces the accuracy.
Even if the approach that was mentioned in the video would be used, i.e. generate and execute python programs for the questions than this would solve only part of the questions, quote from the paper: "Upon manual inspection, 92% and 83% of the programs from GSM8K and MATH genuinely demonstrate the correct reasoning process required to solve the original questions. ".
IMO "Discover AI" should seriously consider reducing the publishing frequency and create more high-quality videos.

author: Tanmay Garg
content:
Hey devs! 👋 Just wanted to share a cool project I've been working on.

> **TL;DR**: I made a tool that cuts OpenAI API costs by 50%+ using their Batch API. It's open-source and dead simple to use.

The backstory: My startup was bleeding cash on OpenAI API calls. I discovered their Batch API (way cheaper!), but using it meant rewriting our whole system. So, I built Batch-GPT – a middleware that does all the batching magic behind the scenes.

Key features:
• 💰 50%+ cost savings
• 🔌 Drop-in replacement for OpenAI clients
• 🧠 Auto-caching for repeat queries
• 🛠️ Resumes interrupted batches

How easy is it? This easy:

```python
client = OpenAI(
 ...,
 base_url="http://batch-gpt/v1"
)
# Rest of your code stays the same!
```

I've open-sourced it here: [https://github.com/tanmay17061/batch-gpt] and written a more detailed blogpost about it here: [https://tanmay17061.medium.com/batch-gpt-we-slashed-our-openai-api-costs-by-over-50-bfbfbabd4e03]

**Would love your thoughts!**

author: killerstorm
content:
There are text embedding model which can transform e.g. 512 tokens into a single 1024-dimensional vector. They are trained specifically to preserve as much semantic information as possible.  E.g. one of papers demonstrated that 32 tokens can be recovered from an embedding vector _exactly_

Suppose a LLM is extended to be able to attend to such embeddings. Now we have a choice: when we add a documented to a context, we can either add it directly as tokens, or compute an embedding and provide these embedding array. In the later case, assuming we use 512 tokens for each embedding, for new token generation we have 512 times less data to attend to.

So it can be a way of compressing the context. Not unlike compressive transformer. I know there were many papers about adding memory to transformers, or compressing contexts, etc. What I want to do differently is to start with existing high-performance models and try to make them work together instead of training it from scratch.

If you'd like to present a paper, send a direct message to <@178150411545346049> with a link to the paper and availability information.


**Paper:**
https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.05954

**Abstract:**
>      Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human objectives is crucial for real-world applications. However, fine-tuning LLMs for alignment often suffers from unstable training and requires substantial computing resources. Test-time alignment techniques, such as prompting and guided decoding, do not modify the underlying model, and their performance remains dependent on the original model's capabilities. To address these challenges, we propose aligning LLMs through representation editing. The core of our method

>      One of the grand challenges of artificial general intelligence is developing agents capable of conducting scientific research and discovering new knowledge. author: Fehir
content:
No one is going to hire you, because you have cert by itself. The "big" (AWS, GCP, Azure) certs show you how to do stuff within their tech stack, so for more engineering roles they are more relevant than vs science/analyst roles. 

With a strong project you can demonstrate actual knowledge, because you can talk about challenges and how did you solve them, which is by far more relevant to hiring managers than saying - I memorized enough to pass this test. As mentioned by liebert, if you actually want to learn something I would steer away from cert and look into coursera (cheaper) or some more expensive options, that are also even better imo.

author: BluBb_mADe
content: But as endo already said, training speed is already bottlenecked on memory with current gddr6x. I think your average large transformer training is only capable of loading the gpu to 50% because of memory latency bandwidth constraints. So having memory that is an order of magnitude slower would mean that your gpu is maybe loaded at 1%. As I said, for small scale training where anything is better than nothing this might be worth it but for any kind of large-ish gpu cluster this is not useful.

author: mark-kh∇
content:
Join us as we chat with Jiawei Zhao on his ground breaking research on Gradient Low-Rank Projection (GaLore), a training strategy that allows full-parameter learning but is more memory-efficient than common low-rank adaptation methods such as LoRA. This approach reduces memory usage by up to 65.5% in optimizer states while maintaining both efficiency and performance for pre-training on LLaMA 1B and 7B architectures with C4 dataset with up to 19.7B tokens, and on fine-tuning RoBERTa on GLUE tasks
We'll also dive into a new training algorithm called Incremental Low-Rank Learning (InRank), which explicitly expresses cumulative weight updates as low-rank matrices while incrementally augmenting their ranks during training.
Jiawei Zhao is a PhD candidate at Caltech, advised by Anima Anandkumar. During his PhD, he also spent most of his time as an intern at NVIDIA Research. He is interested in bridging hardware constraints with the principles of learning in neural networks. Specifically, he f

author: chiphuyen
content:
hi everyone, the schedule is out 🚀 

**[12:00] Crash course on GPU optimization (Mark Saroufim @ Meta)**
Mark is a PyTorch core developer and cofounder of CUDA MODE. Previously, he was at Graphcore.

Mark will give an overview of why GPUs, the metrics that matter, and different GPU programming models (thread-based CUDA and block-based Triton). He promises this will be a painless guide to writing CUDA/Triton kernels.

**[12:45] High-performance LLM serving on GPUs (Sharan Chetlur @ NVIDIA)**
Sharan is a principal engineer working on TensorRT-LLM at NVIDIA. He’s optimized the performance of deep learning models from a single GPU to a full data center scale. Previously, he was at Cerebras.

Sharan will discuss how to build performant, flexible solutions to optimize LLM serving given the rapid evolution of new models and techniques. The talk will cover optimization techniques such as token concatenation, different strategies for batching, and cache.

**Abstract:**
>      Genomic (DNA) sequences encode an enormous amount of information for gene regulation and protein synthesis. Similar to natural language models, researchers have proposed foundation models in genomics to learn generalizable features from unlabeled genome data that can then be fine-tuned for downstream tasks such as identifying regulatory elements. Due to the quadratic scaling of attention, previous Transformer-based genomic models have used 512 to 4k tokens as context (<0.001% of the human genome), significantly limiting the modeling of long-range interactions

author: vitaminc
content:
humans are not designed for high volume mechanical processes, so most automation tools are not designed/built as humanoids.

for small volume mechanical processes, its lower cost to plan and configure the production lines. thus, when you place an order w/ oem, they will typically quote you with an moq.

for ad-hoc mechnical processes, human labors are either lower cost or much higher skilled than any robots today. for example, bespoke tailors, blacksmith, nurses, doctors, etc, with a lot more skills required in addition to just mechanical.

those 'humanoid robots' would have to be at DJI drones type of cost for anything to make sense.

author: Xela
content:
**Project Name: ** SVGFusion - Generating SVGs with Diffusion Models
**Problem Statement**: SVGs are an integral part of modern technology, and Diffusion Models, despite the huge recent advancements in the field, still struggle with SVGs. All of the popular ones, Dall-E and Stable Diffusion etc., just can't produce SVGs.
**Description of the Solution**: We propose SVGFusion, a modified diffusion architecture for generating various SVGs and create a proof of concept model.
**Venture Backend**: No
**Link**: https://xela.blog/posts/svgfusion-mini/#svgfusion

content: hello all. Glad to be here.  I'm working on the storytelling, world building and narrative/game-hosting side of LLMs. My approach to the products I create is that every other type of generative media starts with text prompts, and text generators are great at writing prompts. author: pheela
content:
Hi, I wondered if someone here might be able to give me a pointer on this method I can't find background info for. I am looking to do generative open domain Q&A and while researching how do people fine-tune their LLMs for similar purposes I have stumbled upon something interesting:

This is sample code for sentiment-analysis, my understanding is that the training objective calculates loss based on the answer, while clasically for generative models the objective is next-token prediction.
https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinGPT/blob/master/FinGPT_Training_LoRA_with_ChatGLM2_6B_for_Beginners.ipynb
Classical
```
input = "<Prompt><EOS><Answer><pad_batch>"
output = model(input)
loss = cross-entropy(output[:-1], input[1:])
where "<Prompt>" = "<Question>\n<Context>"
```
FinGPT
```
input = "<Prompt><EOS><Answer><pad_batch>"
output = model(input)
label = "<pad_prompt><EOS><Answer><pad_batch>"
loss = cross-entropy(output, label)
```

This does make sense, I just wondered where the inspira

author: Hell
content:
Material Breakdown: You begin by dissecting any given material into arbitrary chunk sizes. This step is crucial for dealing with GPT's input limitations, ensuring that the material is segmented into manageable pieces for optimal question generation.

Optimization of Chunk Sizes: Through experimentation, you determine the minimal chunk size that allows for the maximum yield of quality questions from GPT-3.5 Turbo. This optimization ensures efficient use of the model's capabilities.

Question Generation: Leveraging the optimized chunks, you generate a comprehensive set of questions. This expansive question set aims to cover the breadth and depth of the material's content.

Answer Generation Using Vector Search and GPT-4: You employ a combination of vector search and GPT-4, enhanced by a carefully crafted prompt that encapsulates the material's essence, to generate answers for the questions. This step ensures high-quality, contextually relevant answers by directing the model's focus.

Hello everyone!

I have created a library in Golang, which enables the creation of a runtime environment for high-performance agents. The core idea is to be able to take any model, make many steps with agents, trying out and reverting to the points where the agent began performing meaningless or erroneous operations, but ultimately obtaining a statistically significant number of "paths" the agent took to achieve the desired result, and extract them for subsequent fine-tuning. Currently, I am exploring  an automatic prompt-breeder - something which would allow an agent's instructions to be modified by another agent, and likewise fine-tune the agent prompt breeder subsequently.

In my experiments, I use Mistral7B and its fine-tunes, simply because it's fast and often provides the correct result, but the scheme works well with models like llama-2. At the moment, I am using 4xA6000 and M2 Max, as the runtime environment supports arbitrary compute resources. I haven't implemented batch infe

author: Martiyay
content:
A appears to use mixed-precision training with float16 data type and parallelization on a GPU. This might lead to faster training times if the model and data are suitable for mixed-precision training and the GPU has the required hardware support.


And 
B involves quantization with 4-bit weights, which can lead to model size reduction and potentially faster inference times on hardware that supports quantized inference.

author: Martiyay
content: And Quantization

author: kapil
content:
Trying to understand Why A is faster than B

A. 

tokenizer_large = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(f"google/flan-t5-large")
model_large = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained(f"google/flan-t5-large", torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map="auto")
model_large = model_large.to('cuda')
model_large.parallelize() 

IS FASTER THEN

B.

model_id = "google/flan-t5-large"

quantization_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=False
)

model_large = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained(model_id, quantization_config=quantization_config)
tokenizer_large = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)

author: Kyrtin
content:
It has no actual understanding or intelligence. You can trick it with a virtually infinite number of trivial jailbreaks. See the recent indirect prompt injections paper for the grim cybersecurity situation that comes with those systems. 

Strong AI requires the dynamics of the only (arguably) general intelligence we have, humans. That requires emotion-based motivation, which play a key role in human decision-making. It also requires the ability to store data with the rich context and connectome of the human brain, which graph databases can do a reasonable job of. Combine the two and you can have emotional values on every surface of the graph, giving a rich motivation landscape that can shift and adapt over time. Combine all of that with a working cognitive architecture and you can get a system that leans human-like concepts in a human-like way.

If you have all of that you have a system that can grow and learn dynamically, quickly, efficiently, and remain robust to manipulation over ti

author: Kyrtin
content: Yeah, you have to examine the definitions at a population level by their characteristics, because effectively having a different definition for every person is like having a literal population of concepts. However, that approach makes the population-level definition vulnerable to adversarial attack via trends of manipulation. The middle-ground is anchoring the weighting of a definition toward what that definition previously meant to maintain consistency, only yielding if a new definition is maintained over an extended period. That approach also mirrors the psychological resistance humans have to constantly changing definitions.

**Paper:**
https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.02643

**Abstract:**
> We introduce the Segment Anything (SA) project: a new task, model, and dataset for image segmentation. Using our efficient model in a data collection loop, we built the largest segmentation dataset to date (by far), with over 1 billion masks on 11M licensed and privacy respecting images. The model is designed and trained to be promptable, so it can transfer zero-shot to new image distributions and tasks. We evaluate its capabilities on numerous tasks and find that its zero-shot performance is impressive -- often competitive with or even superior to prior fully supervised results.

> Recent advances in neural algorithmic reasoning with graph neural networks (GNNs) are propped up by the notion of algorithmic alignment. Specifically, GNNs are claimed to align with dynamic programming (DP), a general problem-solving strategy which expresses many polynomial-time algorithms. author: Vast Abyss
content: I saw that video and thought that it was well put together (like all of his videos).  In my opinion, cryptocurrency can be a great way to decentralize power and democratize the economy, but there are some huge issues we need to address.  First of all, I think that cryptocurrency can't be widely accepted as a digital currency without government enforcement.  Second, I think that cryptocurrencies (like all currencies) should be stable and lose a little bit of value over time.  Third, I think that we need to rethink proof of computation as this centralizes power in those who have the most computing power while also damaging the environment.  Fourth, we need to make transactions quick and reliable.  And finally, we need to come of with an anonymous tax system that is fully autonomous while promoting at least some economic equity.

https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04839

**Abstract:**
> We identity a by-far-unrecognized problem of Adam-style optimizers which results from unnecessary coupling between momentum and adaptivity. The coupling leads to instability and divergence when the momentum and adaptivity parameters are mismatched. In this work, we propose a method, Laprop, which decouples momentum and adaptivity in the Adam-style methods. We show that the decoupling leads to greater flexibility in the hyperparameters and allows for a straightforward interpolation between the signed gradient methods and the adaptive gradient methods. We experimentally show that Laprop has consistently improved speed and stability over Adam on a variety of tasks. We also bound the re

author: Yannic Kilcher
content:
yes, there are better databases, but in the big picture that matters little. for now we could use text files. the initial goal is to go MVP as fast as possible, and many many years of engineering have taught me the following fundamental law about choosing databases:
use postgres, unless you absolutely, positively know that it's not possible with postgres

https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.02155

> Making language models bigger does not inherently make them better at following a user's intent. For example, large language models can generate outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or simply not helpful to the user. In other words, these models are not aligned with their users. In this paper, we show an avenue for aligning language models with user intent on a wide range of tasks by fine-tuning with human feedback. Starting with a set of labeler-written prompts and prompts submitted through the OpenAI API, we collect a dataset of labeler demonstrations of the desired model behavior, which we use to fine-tune GPT-3 using supervised learning. author: ZickZack
content:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.03041
Is a cool paper about foreground-background separation (live demo on huggingface: https://huggingface.co/spaces/ECCV2022/dis-background-removal)
Has some issues, especially once you feed in slightly OOD images (like text) and has some more fundamental issues, like applying it to an already separated image is not a no-op (which is a general red flag for foreground-background separation).
However, it does very well on challenging kind-of naturalistic scenes (like my wallpaper collection), even if no obvious unique separator exists.

author: Lucas Nestler (ClashLuke)
content:
Specifically regarding few-shot performance, it'd make sense to either train the model on the few-shot task, so that it gets better at "understanding" the prompts, or allow fine-tuning as in t-few, which outperforms GPT-3 with 0.1% of the compute and 10% of the memory: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.05638>. 
Our current models seem to largely ignore the prompt and get better with increased number of samples, regardless of their relevance: <https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Do-Prompt-Based-Models-Really-Understand-the-of-Webson-Pavlick/9ba50f992ccd92f428503ea6246157260a26cd77> I even replicated these findings myself, by feeding the model good (german) summaries as the target answer but unrelated english, chinese or german text as the query. Simply by increasing the number of examples from 4 good ones to 8 highly misleading ones, I was able to improve performance and consistency drastically.

author: Yannic Kilcher
content:
Hey everyone (not tagging @ everyone on purpose 😉 ). Soft-launching http://feed.ykilcher.com (redirects to heroku app). Allows you to:
a) compose a thread of up to N (=5 for now) tweets to write a paper summary about a paper / blog post / whatever while providing also some screenshots of main figures
or b) re-tweet an existing tweet and providing a short tl;dr

all entries are manually reviewed (so chill if it doesn't appear right away) and published at https://twitter.com/yk_feed

all tweets & re-tweets must be about research, ideally papers or blog posts that are written like papers. most other things will be declined. Ideally, people will want to follow this account to discover interesting things, and other people will want to post to it to get stuff they find cool discovered by other people. and yes, mode a) also has the ability to circumvent cvpr-like social media bans as it is completely anonymous.

this is pre-alpha version, so expect a few bugs and improvements

> Recent work has demonstrated that increasedtraining   dataset   diversity   improves   generalcross-domain knowledge and downstream gen-eralization capability for large-scale languagemodels.    With  this  in  mind,  we  presentthePile:    an  825  GiB  English  text  corpus  tar-geted  at  training  large-scale  language  mod-els.   The  Pile  is  constructed  from  22  diversehigh-quality subsets—both existing and newlyconstructed—many of which derive from aca-demic  or  professional  sources.    Our  evalua-tion of the untuned performance of GPT-2 andGPT-3  on  the  Pile  shows  that  these  modelsstruggle on many of its components,  such asacademic writing. Hello everyone, 

I am a researcher in the field of visual analytics. My goal with Flatland competition is to help the participants in the analysis process of the data generated from the environment. I am not participating in the competition, but trying to make a visual analysis tool that is useful for the community. To this end, since you are actively participating in the competition, I need to understand your perspective, current analysis methods, and process of interpreting behavior/performance of trained agents. It would help me a lot if we can schedule an individual meeting (~35 min) to talk and understand your perspective/requirements. I plan to build an analysis tool and make it available to the community in the hope that it helps you in return. I would be grateful if you can spend some time for the discussion. Please send me a message on Discord or write me an email (shivam.agarwal@paluno.uni-due.de) and then we can schedule the meeting.
